a.xlink:title: Defines the parameterized function that maps tokens to next-token probabilities. Under Parameter Golf, architecture choices must trade off accuracy against strict parameter and memory ceilings.
Maps discrete tokens into continuous vectors the network can process. Embeddings often dominate parameter count in tiny LLMs because they scale with vocabulary size.
Stacks attention and feed-forward blocks to model complex token dependencies. Under Parameter Golf, most innovation lives in reallocating parameter/memory across these sub-components.
Maps final hidden states to logits over the vocabulary. The output layer can match embedding size and may rival embeddings in parameter cost unless weights are tied or factorized.
Defines how token IDs are converted to vectors and how much parameter budget is allocated to that mapping. Changing this is a common way to free parameters for the backbone.
Injects token position information so the model can distinguish order. Some methods are parameter-free but affect attention behavior and memory scaling with context length.
A learned lookup table mapping each vocabulary token to a dense vector. It is expressive but can consume a large fraction of the parameter budget.
Represents embeddings as a low-rank factorization to reduce parameters while preserving expressivity. This trades some embedding capacity for parameter headroom.
Maps multiple tokens to shared embedding slots via hashing or sharing rules to reduce parameters. This can free significant budget but may introduce collisions that hurt accuracy.
Applies a deterministic rotation to query/key vectors based on position, enabling extrapolation to longer contexts. It is parameter-free and common in modern LLMs.
Adds a head-dependent linear bias to attention logits that encodes relative positions. It is parameter-free and can improve long-context generalization under budget.
Uses a learned embedding for each position up to the context limit. It adds parameters proportional to context length and can be less robust to extrapolation.
A repeated unit combining attention, MLP, normalization, and residual connections. Its internal design determines both parameter efficiency and activation/KV-cache memory.
Computes content-dependent mixing of token representations to model long-range interactions. Attention is memory-heavy due to attention logits/activations and KV cache during inference.
Applies per-token nonlinear transformations that expand and contract hidden representations. MLP layers often dominate parameter count and a large share of activation memory.
Stabilizes activations and gradients to enable deep training. Normalization has low parameter cost but strongly affects optimization dynamics in small models.
Adds skip connections that improve gradient flow and allow iterative refinement of representations. Residuals add negligible parameters but contribute to activation storage.
Randomly zeroes activations during training to reduce overfitting. It has no parameters but can change optimization behavior and effective capacity in tiny models.
Linear layers that produce queries, keys, values, and output projections. Projection parameter count scales roughly with hidden size squared, making it a major budget lever.
Computes attention weights and applies them to values. The choice of implementation affects peak activation memory and speed, especially at longer context lengths.
Stores past keys/values for autoregressive decoding to avoid recomputation. KV cache memory scales with context length and head dimensions, often dominating inference memory.
Projects hidden states into query vectors used to retrieve information from keys/values. Its parameters scale with hidden size and number of layers.
Projects hidden states into key vectors that index information in attention. Key projection can be per-head (more parameters) or shared across heads (fewer parameters).
Projects hidden states into value vectors that carry content aggregated by attention weights. Like keys, values can be per-head or shared to reduce parameters and KV cache.
Projects concatenated head outputs back to the model hidden size. It is required for standard multi-head attention and scales with hidden size squared.
Computes distinct key vectors for each attention head, maximizing expressivity. This typically uses more parameters than shared-key variants.
Shares key projections across heads or head groups, reducing parameter count. It often trades some expressivity for large memory savings in KV cache during inference.
Computes distinct value vectors per head to maximize representational capacity. This uses more parameters and increases KV cache size compared to shared-value variants.
Shares value projections across heads or head groups, reducing parameter count. It can significantly reduce inference KV cache memory at small parameter budgets.
Determines how attention logits and softmax are computed and stored. Memory-efficient implementations can reduce peak activation memory without changing parameters.
Materializes attention logits/weights more directly, increasing activation memory at long context. It is widely supported but often memory-limited in Parameter Golf settings.
Computes attention in tiles to avoid storing full attention matrices. This can sharply reduce peak activation memory while preserving standard softmax attention.
Does not store past keys/values, recomputing them each step. This reduces memory but increases compute and can be infeasible for long contexts.
Stores keys/values in half precision, balancing fidelity and memory usage. This is a common baseline for inference memory accounting.
Quantizes KV cache to 8-bit representations to reduce memory footprint. This can introduce approximation error but may be critical under strict memory budgets.
Chooses the internal structure of the MLP (activation, gating, sparsity, or factorization). This is a prime target for parameter reallocation toward OOD ideas.
Standard two-layer feed-forward network with a pointwise nonlinearity. It is a strong baseline but uses many parameters for a given hidden size.
Uses a gated activation that can improve quality at fixed depth. It often increases parameters slightly versus a dense FFN but can be more compute-efficient per gain.
Factorizes large MLP matrices into low-rank components to reduce parameters. This can free budget for attention or new modules but may bottleneck capacity.
Routes tokens to a subset of expert MLPs, increasing total parameters without proportional compute. It can boost capacity but complicates routing and memory accounting.
Normalizes activations using mean and variance across hidden dimensions with learned gain/bias. It is widely used but can be slower than RMSNorm in some kernels.
Normalizes by root-mean-square without subtracting the mean, typically using only a learned gain. It can be more parameter- and compute-efficient while remaining stable.
Defines how logits are produced, including whether the head is untied, tied to embeddings, or structured to reduce parameters. This is a common place to free parameters under strict budgets.
Uses a separate weight matrix to map hidden states to vocabulary logits. This maximizes flexibility but can nearly double parameters when embeddings are also large.
Reuses the token embedding matrix as the output projection, reducing parameters and sometimes improving generalization. This is a high-leverage Parameter Golf trick.
Structures the output distribution to reduce compute and parameters for rare tokens. It can save parameters but adds complexity and may affect benchmark comparability.
